text_content: I can believe Jason got arrested. A person who lived two houses down from me had a wolf dog hybrid. He had frequently broken state laws regarding enclosures for wolf dogs. The dog almost attacked me once. It was killing small animals including a neighbor's cat. Then one night another neighbors sister and brother-in-law were leaving their house, and somehow their car keys had fallen out of a jacket. The sister-in-law was holding their baby by the car door waiting for her husband to find the keys when they heard a growl. The wolf dog had her cornered and she couldn't get in the car. My neighbor fired two shots in the air, then one close to the wolf dog, but it never backed away so finally he felt he had no choice but to shoot it. He was charged, but he reached a settlement with the owner of the wolf dog and courts where he had to pay for medical bills, and court costs.  He was facing a felony, but he was a bailiff and if he didn't take the deal, he would have most likely lost his job.  This was even though it was found out the dogs owner had left the dog there for a week while he went to visit his girlfriend, but just left a big pile of food and a bucket of water in the enclosure. The dog probably ate all the food within the first couple of days and was starving. Nobody blamed the dog, including the neighbor who shot him. We all blamed the dogs owner plus whoever was in charge of fines or assessing those fines to the owner when he had repeatedly broken the law.  That's the biggest issue in my opinion. Their biggest threat is the pet trade but this video almost sounds like a commercial for getting one as a pet. 

Their second threat as is every animals is habitat loss. I think there should be a global law that requires that all use on any land that is now in use can only be expanded up or downwards and that humans should not be allowed to take resource or use any land that requires areas outside the perimeter we use. So if we are cutting down trees, it would have to stop exactly where the last tree fell. If we need more trees than we need to convert some other land that is already in use by humans. I also think it would be a nice addition to my made up global law that we need to relinquish a certain amount of land area that we are already using back permanently back to nature each year. The relinquished land back to nature must be in great condition and all traces of human contamination should have be cleaned up and removed. 

I always want everything for a pet but I would never ever do that. However, despite being so little and cute, I wouldn’t want one or a troop of these. I don’t like the way they move their heads so fast, stops before I would expect it to, and they constantly move their head and in so many different directions. They could just have moved their eyes instead. It makes them like shifty. Plus I was a little grossed out by the way they eat tree sap. No need for stretching the sap back and forth, thin to thick. Their twitchy little critters.

text_content: Because of their "cuteness", videos of pet slow lorises are some of the mostly frequently watched animal-related viral videos on YouTube.[60][123] In March 2011, a newly posted video of a slow loris holding a cocktail umbrella had been viewed more than two million times, while an older video of a slow loris being tickled had been viewed more than six million times.[131] According to Nekaris, these videos are misunderstood by most people who watch them, since most do not realize that it is illegal in most countries to own them as pets and that the slow lorises in the videos are only docile because that is their passive defensive reaction to threatening situations.[123][131] Despite frequent advertisements by pet shops in Japan, the World Conservation Monitoring Centre reported only a few dozen slow lorises were imported in 2006, suggesting frequent smuggling.[83] Slow lorises are also smuggled to China, Taiwan, Europe, Russia, the United States, and Saudi Arabia for use as pets.[130][120][131]

This story should also serve as an incentive to demand that _all_ "animal breeders" be recognized as the evil @$$holes they truly are.

Animal "breeding" should be *_harshly_* regulated, if even permitted at all, regardless of which species is  held captive. 

They should all be subjected to frequent unnanounced checks by veterinarians and law enforcement to ensure they can't do to even one more bird or other animal what was done to Victoria the Cockatoo.

If any "breeder" is found to be abusing, neglecting, and forcing any animal to subsist in suboptimal conditions, the animals should be immediately seized and evacuated to safety, and the captor charged with every single violation and ruthlessly prosecuted. 

And one such epidode should permanently preclude them from EVER having any more animals in their clutches ever again, either. 

*I do not care one iota* about the "Oh, poor me, it's my livelihood!" whining as an excuse to abuse, torture, deprive, and neglect other species held as *_slaves,_* forced to live in misery each and every day because some lazy, greedy, and reckless @$$hole is using animal misery to make money.

Screw that, and screw them. 

Those types deserve to suffer the way they make other beings suffer, and I don't care who's feelings get hurt reading this, either. 

If you make your money on the backs of captive slaves, then _you deserve to suffer equal consequences for your crimes._

Victoria's life was significantly shortened due the abuse and torture she suffered. 

_There is no excuse that will ever suffice,_ no second chances deserved, no mercy extended, and no f*cks given about @$$holes who treat animals the way Victoria was treated. 

I just can't care about anyone who whines about how they they are "overegulated" by authorities when they are greedy, abusive pieces of 💩!

video_title: Want A BABY TURTLE? 🐢 WATCH THIS! ⚠️
text_content: I had my yellow slider for 15 years! I got them when I was 8 for a birthday present they were as small as a 50p my grandma only thought they got as big as a £5 note and live for 5-7 years and was given a small pink fish tank😅 15 years later they were as big as a small melon and we just ran out of room for them and as covid hit and fish stores closed feeding them was hard I lived in England and at that point terrapin were illegal to sell so after covid lock down ended I started looking for sanctuaries! text_content: I appreciate that you pointed out that there are plenty of these turtles already in need of homes. One thing I wish you had added was the reason why these turtles are actually illegal in some places. Here in the Pacific Northwest, red eared-sliders are invasive, competing against our native painted and western pond turtles. People will either intentionally let their pets loose, or they will put their pets in a pond or stream in their yard, and it's not too hard for them to find their way to other bodies of water. Like you said, they are really hardy reptiles, which unfortunately makes them really good at establishing invasive populations, so it is important for people to be aware of just how hardy (and potentially harmful) they can be.

text_content:
I'm Brazilian and if this turtle genuinely came from the Amazon forest and was not raised in captivity it's possible that it was illegally trafficked. In Brazil, the sale and possession of Mata-Mata turtles are illegal according to the Environmental Crimes Law (Law No. 9,605/1998), and the Brazilian Institute of Environment and Renewable Natural Resources (IBAMA) also regulates the possession and trade of native species. I highly recommend you guys to research where this fish store sourced this turtle!!!

The sale and trade of Mata-Mata turtles for abroad can be legal in some cases, as long as all applicable laws and regulations are followed, both in the country of origin and the destination country. To legally export a Mata-Mata turtle as a pet, the exporter must obtain a CITES export permit from the country of origin and ensure that all local laws related to the possession and trade of wildlife are respected. In addition, the importer must ensure that the trade and possession of the turtle are legal in the destination country and, if necessary, obtain a CITES import permit or other relevant authorizations. You should ask the pet store for documentation regarding the origin of the Mata-Mata turtle and any required permits, such as CITES import permits or any other relevant authorizations.

video_title: Ball Pythons Are NOT The Best Beginner PET Snake! | THIS IS!
video_description: Most people automatically recommend ball pythons as the best beginner snake. But after years of keeping both species, many ...
text_content:
My first snake's a rosy boa and he's wonderful; docile just like his rainforest relative, he eats readily, and every time I take him out he's enthusiastically exploring. But I also need to state that I'm in Canada where rosy boas are *extremely difficult to find because almost nobody breeds them here* and that it took me years to find him. They are RARE snakes here! Here in Canada, there's *nothing* common about my little stripey boy!

Meanwhile, Boa imperator is stupidly illegal in my province, so I couldn't adopt Churro's big cousin if I wanted to. And I didn't want to because right now I still find the scale of even adult ball pythons intimidating. Meanwhile I can scoop up Churro one handed, and because he's smaller, it's easier for me to give him an epic enclosure that is much bigger than his species' stated minimum.
